author: @glencahalin4786
text:
Must admit that at the moment I fall into the "no room" category, so tend to fill up the useable (head) space with thought experiments, which I'll tend to throw out and see if anyone want to give them a go.

Today's one is due to having looked through your back catalogue and suddenly been struck by some advantages of sticking a Whirlybird on top of one of your Darwin generator designs, as it creates a lower pressure to "suck" air up that should increase airflow through and hence the rate of turn of the internal generator.  I think it's also possibly to use the Whirly part for it's own (  rim ) generation.  My mind says that this is a kind of push-you pull-me effect, as the Darwin would also be creating a higher pressure to turn the Whirlybird. The end result being that either/both should start turning and producing energy at slightly lower wind speeds than either alone.

My bent, currently, seems to be "how could one produce something/anything from ( common) situations that normally produce nothing"

author: @ArtisticImpressionsbyBobRouth
text: Rob, great video. I have a thought about what you said. Just watching you do the fundamentals to getting an answer to the questions that the viewers have. Those questions are individual to each of the viewers. I watch your programs as soon as they hit my Que. But I'm retired and I would guess we are about the same age. I find so many new and interesting things I didn't know by your programs. I learn more than anything, the knowledge of where to go to find the answer when my project intersects your programming. I am amazed how often you are my go to guy because of the huge library of knowledge you have collected and offered to the world. Bravo professor. I said before you are becoming a College of knowledge for the inquisitive. Offering a degree in Applied Sciences.  Thank you so very much.  Just working the mind and brain is at my level exciting.

text: The Tao of Rob

author: @clint9344
text: Robert, from a viewers point of view.... It is the way you present and teach something to others also. Sometimes you have to make and show someone an idea for it to get improved upon. You have a way of doing just that. Your a Natural Borne Teacher. You have a way of presenting and breaking topics down that others do not. So yes no NEW KNOWLEDGE..its how its presented and given to the masses. You take everyday needs and find a way to reach them with just a lil effort and KNOWLEDGE.. You make others want to go out and experiment. I can honestly say at the moment I live vicariously thru you. You pointed it out... WE HAVE TO DO IT.. You have made it easier for us to get off our Keisters and go for it. You deserve to be commended for your Inspiration to your viewers.  Now the next issue is to solve is Hoarding...lol.  Be in peace God speed.

text: Also, big difference between knowledge and application of knowledge.🤔

author: @grendel1960a
text: as an inveterate tinkerer my interests bounce around from one thing to another, the core behind all of them is using the machines I have to make stuff, it doesnt matter what stuff it is, as long as I am making I am happy. timewise, you dont need to spend much time doing these things, just try and do a little bit every day- I have been building a model boat, when I started it was just 20 minutes a day, just get one bit done, and no, its still not quite complete 5 years later, plus I dont work on it every day, but I do work on something every day, those 15 - 20 minutes in the workshop (some days it can stretch to hours) keep me happy, they are my time to spend thinking only of the project in hand, where the stresses of the day just disappear. If you are unsure where to start, start anywhere, make something you are not going to need until later, start at the beginning - or not, but the important part is that you have started - then do a bit more next day- now you are making progress. If it takes an hour to complete- or if it takes 5 years is unimportant, as you will be doing.

author: @frankfaubert1927
text:
The entire issue with "nothing new here" is that you don't know what you don't know. 

Perhaps the commenter didn't find anything new but there were probably hundreds if not thousands of viewers who did learn something new.

Imagine a world where we don't do things simply because one person thinks they've got it all sussed.

On the other side of the coin, I have a reasonably good background in electronics, coding, CAD/CAM, CNC construction and a host of trades skills and I'm always learning new things from this channel.

author: @LawrenceMarks27
text: I remember googling some subject trying to understand wind generation better.  When I got to your link, it wasn't even necessarily that you were saying something new.  It was that you were saying something in a manner that just spoke to my mind.  Ten people can say the exact same thing and project a different meaning. What doesn't work for one, may actually work for another, and visa versa.  So... yea.  Oh, and I've rewatched a few of your videos at least a half dozen times.... nothing new every time. :)

author: @Alkimi
text:
Hehe... "you're not going to be able to bake that cake" 😂 🫢ZING!🤭😏 
Personally, I am ENDLESSLY inspired by everything you say here. Just being reminded daily that we think so much alike and that you are such a prolific artist is invaluable to me. That's why I love the internet. Comments like that, among other things, are the reason I hate the internet. It's the ultimate love/hate relationship. 🤣

Never stop, brother! 🫡

There are newcomers every day and they will discover the fires from the sparks you create.  They are just blinkered and nee to frolick unbridled for a while.

All quite interesting to me so thank you. Hoping to send you the stl files on a hybridized version oof two of your designs when it is working as i like

author: @pedjamilosavljevic6235
text:
I still get triggered , when Rob says - I found it in a bin. That's because where I live , it would be for sale on some selling platforms (for private individuals). But , it is not Rob's fault.
 On the topic of "no new knowledge here" - well , the person who wrote it must be a genius and have a photographic memory. I've seen , probably , more than 90% of all Rob's videos and there were times , when I was thinking to myself , why would he explain things , that are a common knowledge (like molar solutions and Avogadro's number) ? Then , after some deflation of my arrogance , I remembered that many of my (secondary and high) school friends couldn't remember what it is , if their life depends on it and that different countries have a different curriculum in schools. Also , there were some things on this channel , that I was reminded about and many more things that I didn't know at all. Of course , that is not a NEW knowledge per say , but it's new to ME , therefore , it has a value (and cost me nothing , but some time). 
 Internet is full of all sorts of people and , since this channel is pretty old (in internet terms it is ancient) , there are always a lot of new viewers , which don't get what is this channel about (even though Rob has explained it several times - people didn't saw particular videos). Also , we live in such times , where many people want instantaneous gratification , from whatever they are watching , so there will be some people , who are disappointed , because Rob didn't gave them precise instruction how to get energy independence , or how to make their own (commercial like) batteries , for penny's . That's how it goes..

author: @voltrevolt8731
text: So very well said. The cliche "curiosity killed the cat" ignores that the cat has 8 additional lives in which to be curious. And how often does curiosity actually kill a cat? They seem to usually land on their feet really. Seriously, though, the most interesting things I've stumbled across have been totally accidental while pursuing a preconceived objective, and as you say, these lead to new avenues of inquiry. I'm 99.5% clueless about all of this stuff, but that's no hindrance -- occasionally, it can even be an advantage.

author: @KimberlyRPeacock
text: No new knowledge here is like shouting no imagination.  You might as well shout to every engineer,no new knowledge here because they don’t make scientific discoveries, they take scientific discoveries and design products based on them.

author: @TomFarrell-p9z
text: Very interesting!  All the more so because I'm staring at a Mr Coffee espresso machine that was gifted to us.  (Turns out the effort to approximate a Starbuck's vanilla latte isn't worth it to me.)  But Robert, my problem is how do I store all the odd parts until I'm ready to use them?  I literally have a basement full, and forget what I have.  Would love it if you would make a video about how you deal with this!

replies.author: @jeffrice6745
replies.text: label it and keep a list!

replies.author: @willhibbardii2450
replies.text: I have over 100 of 27-gallon storage containers with lids, that I can stack up to 5 high. under each stack is a furniture dolly so they are easy to move around and keep the dust off and out from under.  Each bin should be inventoried and labeled. The space that 5 containers take up for every stack is about 60 cubic feet so overall the floor space footprint is 120 square feet. For 1,200 cubic feet of storage for my collected stuff. It's my valued inventory in my shop that has no value to most people however is a wealth when I need the unobtanium of parts that are no longer available and or discontinued for restorations. I need to create washable covers for each stack. It's a consolidated work in progress.

replies.author: @clint9344

replies.author: @willhibbardii2450
replies.text: @clint9344 In 2014 I was completely downsized... Now the space that I could live in very happily BTW has transformed into a 6,000 Sq. foot home plus a 3,000 Sq ft shop on 2 acres. With a 2-hour drive commute between home and shop. Plus, I have the home with an anchor I had downsized to. Things you do when you fall in love. Farming 40 acres for thirty years was easier than taking care of all this however the people in my life makes it worthwhile and easy. Be in peace and God's speed to you! Happy Thanksgiving!

replies.author: @clint9344
replies.text: @willhibbardii2450 I comprehend. If your happy and they are happy, how can you be wrong. Happy Thanksgiving to you and your family. Be in peace God speed.

author: @Vibe77Guy
text:
I can't begin to talk about how many times I've gone shopping for an item, for a purpose that had nothing at all to do with it's original marketed design purpose.  Many times because I could not find such a thing in a rubbish bin.
Dog food storage containers for chemical tanks for example, simply because it was made of chemical resistant polypropylene, and held the desired volume.
